The other strategy is to write it yourself. So I actually just did this for my fiance, who's a real estate agent, and I'm doing the marketing for her, obviously. And so she sold some houses, and I wanted specific types of testimonials. Let's talk a little bit about that in a second. But you want different types of testimonials. And so I wrote them, and she got her clients to approve them, and they literally approved them all word for word. And it was the easiest thing on the planet. So you need to go through this. You know, when I write copy for clients, I will often write the testimonial that should be here in the copy. Now, you got to go find one that is real, that matches as closely to this as possible. So what are the elements that make up a good testimonial? A great testimonial is, first of all, always some version of before and after. So it is a before and after picture textualized. So it is some version of I was fat, dumb, broke, miserable, unhappy, living in a cardboard box, disdained by everybody. And now I'm always, quote, Richard Pryor's great joke about that was that his before and after is better than anybody else's is I was a poor black guy, and I'm a rich white guy. That's as big a before and after as you can possibly have. But they all have a before and after element to them. Drama helps. So if it's dramatic, if somebody only lost 35 pounds on the diet, but there was drama to it, I've been on 50 diets before. I was contemplating suicide. I was worried my husband was if there's drama to it, then the 35 pounds is the same as if she lost 350 pounds. So drama, details. So a lot of people leave out all the details. And details matter to people. So there's the details of the person, first of all. So.
